•ES went limit-down again Sunday night; the Fed announced unlimited QE pre-market Monday; the cash market still flushed to 2191.86 intraday — the low of the crash — and closed -2.9% at 2237.40.

Next two weeks:

•Mar 24: +9.4%, best day since Oct 2008 (Dow +11.4%, best since 1933)
•Mar 26: +6.2% — the three-day rally off the low exceeded +20% intraday, technically the fastest “new bull market” ever
•Week 1 post-OpEx (Mar 23–27): +10.3%, best week since 2009
•Week 2 was digestion: Apr 1 fell -4.4%, and two weeks out (Apr 3) SPX sat at 2488.65 — +8.0% from the OpEx close, +13.5% from the Monday low. It never looked back.

So for the thread’s purposes: the single most important market turn of the decade occurred on the first trading day after quarterly OpEx. The structural read is clean — enormous put OI expired Friday, dealer short-gamma hedges unwound, charm/vanna tailwinds kicked in, and record quarter-end pension rebalancing buying hit the following week. The market was “unpinned.”

The confound is equally enormous, though: unlimited QE landed the morning of the 23rd and the $2.2T CARES Act was signed the 27th. You can’t cleanly separate gamma mechanics from the largest policy bazooka in history firing the same week. What keeps the pattern interesting is the rhyme: December 2018 also bottomed the Monday after quad witching (Dec 24, then +5% on Dec 26), and June 2022’s local low printed on OpEx Friday itself. Post-quarterly-OpEx windows recurrently host violent turns — March 2020 is just the most extreme specimen.

Sept just March?

tweets.text:
I respect Ansem for publishing the thesis, but I don’t think solana:9cRCn9rGT8V2imeM2BaKs13yhMEais3ruM3rPvTGpump can work in its current form as the sustainable “tokenized creator economy” he describes

The fundamental problem is simple: the proposal tokenizes Ansem’s attention without giving the token a defined claim on the value that attention creates

This is an age old problem in crypto; one that @Blockworks B1 transparency filings or @umia_finance equity structuring aim to solve

i) Existing Commercial Products & Value Accrual 

@blknoiz06 already has several assets capable of monetizing his audience, including Bullpen, Market Bubble, sponsorships and pumpfun creator fees. Yet solana:9cRCn9rGT8V2imeM2BaKs13yhMEais3ruM3rPvTGpump holders appear to receive no ownership, revenue rights, governance or enforceable claim over any of them

If those businesses grow, the economic value accrues to Ansem and the businesses themselves. The token benefits only if his growing influence persuades someone else to buy it at a higher price

Saying that a portion of creator fees may eventually “bolster the ecosystem” does not fix this. There is no defined percentage, binding mechanism or explanation of how those funds create persistent demand for the token

"Discretionary" buybacks or incentives can support price temporarily, but they cannot manufacture durable value capture

ii) Flawed Comparison (Meta) 

The @Meta comparison also falls apart. Meta shareholders own a legally recognized interest in a company with revenues, assets, governance rights and residual economics. Holding solana:9cRCn9rGT8V2imeM2BaKs13yhMEais3ruM3rPvTGpump does not give you ownership of Ansem’s audience, content, businesses or future earnings... It gives you exposure to market sentiment around his personal brand

iii) $2T Creator Economy

The $2T creator economy figure is therefore largely irrelevant. That market represents advertising, subscriptions, products, services and creator-owned businesses. A creator coin receives no share of that activity simply because it exists alongside it. MrBeast generating hundreds of millions in commercial revenue validates creator businesses, not creator tokens

iv) Token supply & liquidity concerns

The supply structure creates another major issue. Ansem owning approximately 58% of the token makes alignment radically asymmetric and introduces an enormous dependency on one person’s future decisions. Staggered airdrops may postpone distribution, but they do not remove dilution or eventual sell pressure

A token inventory marked at the latest market price also cannot be treated as a liquid $100M treasury, because deploying or selling meaningful size affects the market supporting that valuation

v) Community Marketing incentives

The “number go up” acquisition funnel is especially fragile. It works while price appreciation turns holders into marketers, but the loop reverses during a sustained drawdown

Communities built around financial incentives naturally become less active when those incentives disappear. A sustainable social network needs relationships, products and recurring activity that survive weak token performance

vi) Is a token even needed?

There is also no clear reason why protocol partnerships require solana:9cRCn9rGT8V2imeM2BaKs13yhMEais3ruM3rPvTGpump. Ansem can already direct users toward protocols through content, referral programmes, quests, points, sponsorships or product integrations. Paying token holders to use a protocol amounts to subsidized customer acquisition. Retention will still depend on whether the underlying product is useful once the rewards end

Calling the resulting holder base a social network feels premature as well. The actual social graph, content and distribution remain on X, YouTube, Kick, TikTok and other platforms. A wallet list organized around one creator does not automatically become a network with defensible interactions or ownership of the underlying audience

vii) Benchmarked Comparisons

The Friend tech, Time fun and celebrity coin examples point toward this same structural problem... their failures went deeper than fees, timing, poor memes or insufficiently influential creators

Speculation and creator access were bundled together even though the useful parts could be delivered directly, while the token lacked durable value capture once attention moved elsewhere. Adding a larger creator and stronger marketing improves the launch conditions, but it does not resolve that mismatch

TLDR: solana:9cRCn9rGT8V2imeM2BaKs13yhMEais3ruM3rPvTGpump can absolutely perform well as a trade... but there's numerous holes to plug

Ansem has enormous distribution, deep crypto-native reach and enough committed capital to generate a powerful reflexive cycle. DOGE and TRUMP prove that attention can support extraordinary peak valuations, but peak valuation is not evidence of sustainable economics (check their price now)

For this to become the new primitive being described, it needs a real product, transparent treasury rules, defined token rights, enforceable value routing and a reason for users or protocols to hold the asset beyond speculation and rewards

It also needs a network capable of producing value independently of Ansem continuously promoting it

Until those pieces exist, solana:9cRCn9rGT8V2imeM2BaKs13yhMEais3ruM3rPvTGpump is best understood as a creator-backed memecoin with an unusually strong distribution channel. That may still make it successful, but it does not make it a tokenized social network or a credible way to capture the growth of the creator economy

tweets.text:
Feels bad, -49.4% drawdown this month after the recent crash.

My portfolio is mainly AI chokepoints and bottlenecks. 

In the memory, photonics, robotics, and upstream semis, (on margin) which all tend to be higher beta than others. But reduced leverage recently from the crash.

I see a lot of people making fun of the drop or AI names, saying it’s obvious that:

- “AI is a bubble”
- “memory/kospi is a bubble”
- “photonics is a bubble”
- “humanoids won’t get anywhere”
- “neoclouds will get replaced by hyperscalers like Meta”

And a bunch of retail + bots saying “sell everything, it’s never going to recover”.

But I have conviction that all these themes are backed by structural revenue growth or technological shifts.

And I’ve had similar drawdowns back when there admin threatened global tariffs, before markets pulled off a recovery.

I personally have a longer horizon + higher tolerance for volatility than others, to see how this plays out.

Especially considering a lot of retail view things on a week to week basis: no, my thesis isn’t wrong yet if I project revenue inflection in H2 2027 and it’s 2026 now. 

Anyway, feels bad short term just wanted to share anyway for transparency.

Agreed! It’s nice to remember your thesis during a market crash.

From my own personal thesis, if $AAOI hits $1.4B quarterly revenue start of Q3 2027. Which is annualized $5.6B off a $8B MC. 

Is it “over” for the company if that revenue ramp hasn’t even shown up in the quarterly earnings… when it’s 2026?

Same applies to my CPO sector exposure like $SIVE, an architecture shift led by $NVDA. 

If scale out volume ramps from H2 2026 into 2027, and scale up heavily volume ramps into 2028.

Is it “over” that $0 -> $91B TAM expansion (per GS) hasn’t even hit yet?

With robotics like Agility, is it over in 2026 if the listing hasn’t even happened yet and humanoids haven’t ramped?

I personally think current market conditions are a reflection of liquidity and leverage, not individual fundamentals.

It’s brutal for everyone to see KOSPI, TW, Nikkei, and AI, space, robotics sector stocks crash recently. 

Especially when there’s a lot of irrational behavior stemming from those leverage.

In the end, we can’t tell you what stocks to buy, what timeframe you should sell, how to size your positions, or what you should do. 

Only share personal thoughts or research and track if they get validated over time.

So it’s extremely important to build your own thesis, since everyone has unique risk tolerance or investing timeframes.

And that usually leads to having higher conviction during crashes.

tweets.text:
Engineers at Coinbase are getting closer to recursive self-improvement, or loops.

For instance, agents can now ingest and summarize customer feedback collected right in the app, prioritize bugs and features based on that feedback, draft the code, security review it, and provide it to a human engineer for final review. All happens automatically each day and it learns from the human edits to improve over time.

We're finding more areas to introduce loops across the company. Instead of prompting agents with what to do, you can increasingly give them a goal, and they bring back high quality work for you to review.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
The fundamentals of engineering haven't changed, but the daily work has.

We're managing agents, building loops and taste and judgment matter more than ever. 

So we rebuilt how we hire engineers from scratch to match how we build. Here's what we've been up to @ Coinbase:
